reasoning_summary: Chosen patient B because they have been on the waiting list much longer, and although they gain fewer years of life from the transplant, they still have 10 years left, making it the best choice given all else equal.
Chose patient B mainly because of longer waitlist time, despite being overweight. The interviewee was uncertain about the impact of obesity but considered the longer waiting time more significant.

Chose patient A because of longer time on the waiting list and sufficient years of life gained (10 years), despite patient B having more years gained and working more hours. The choice might change if patient A's years of life gained were very low.

The interviewee felt this was a tough choice. They prioritized patient B because although patient B gains fewer years of life, they have been on the waiting list much longer, and dialysis only allows about 5 to 10 years of life. Thus, patient B is seen as being on borrowed time and requires priority.

The interviewee prioritized patient A mainly due to higher number of dependents and better obesity level, which they believe helps preserve the kidney. They did not consider work hours as important, and viewed years on waitlist as similar for both, so dependents and obesity tipped the decision.

The decision was difficult, but the interviewee leaned toward patient A because of having a dependent and a possibly healthier obesity level (underweight vs obese), despite patient B having more years of life to gain. They weighed factors by points and felt patient A won slightly.

Faced with a conflict between number of dependents (favoring B) and years on waiting list (favoring A), interviewee leans toward Patient A because the longer wait time (7 years vs 1 year) should take precedence, despite acknowledging the importance of dependents.

Decision is difficult; Patient A has more dependents, longer expected life gain, and normal weight favoring them, while Patient B has longer wait time and works more hours. Ultimately leans slightly toward A, largely influenced by obesity level assumed to affect survivability, but admits it's almost a toss-up.

Struggles to weigh number of dependents against life expectancy and waitlist time; initially feels inclination for Patient A due to dependents but ultimately chooses Patient B based on significantly greater life expectancy and practical considerations, acknowledging difficulty and accepting computer's likely preference.

Despite patient B having no criminal history, patient A is prioritized because they have 3 dependents. The interviewee weighs the number of dependents higher than the criminal history when dependents outnumber crimes, prioritizing societal impact.

Patient A is chosen due to higher years of life gained (25 vs 20), more dependents (2 vs 1), and normal weight versus overweight. The interviewee does not consider work hours or waitlist time important.

The interviewee prioritizes years of life gained over number of dependents and obesity, choosing patient B who is expected to gain 20 years versus 10 for patient A, despite obesity and fewer dependents.

Although patient A would gain more years of life after transplant, patient B has waited significantly longer on the transplant list. The interviewee prioritizes waitlist time over life expectancy in this conflict, giving the kidney to patient B.

Patient A was chosen mainly because patient B is overweight, which the interviewee considers to negatively affect transplant longevity. The waitlist years and years expected to gain were also considered but weight was the deciding factor.

This scenario was harder to decide. The interviewee considered underweight better than obese and noted patient A has dependents and longer wait time, but ultimately used life expectancy as a tiebreaker, choosing patient B who would gain more years of life despite obesity.

Patient B was prioritized because they have been on the waiting list longer (7 years vs. 1 year), despite Patient A having a higher life expectancy gain. The participant mentally combined waitlist years with life years gained and concluded that B's waiting time outweighed A's longer life expectancy gain.

Participant gave more weight to the length of time on the list and found life expectancy comparable between patients, thus choosing Patient B who has been waiting longer, dismissing differences in dependents, work hours, and obesity as less important factors.

Participant prioritized Patient A due to the longer waitlist time (5 vs. 3 years) despite Patient B having higher life expectancy gain. They considered life expectancy differences under 5 years as comparable and thus gave greater importance to waiting time.

Patient A was chosen mainly because of the longer waitlist time (7 years vs 1 year), indicating a more urgent need due to limited life expectancy. The number and age of dependents for Patient B was acknowledged but was not enough to override the urgency indicated by time on the waitlist.

Though Patient B had a longer waitlist time, Patient A was favored due to more expected years of life gained, more dependents, and a normal weight status compared to Patient B's overweight status. The interviewee expressed some uncertainty but leaned towards Patient A based on likelihood of transplant success.

Patient A was selected despite fewer expected years of life gained and fewer work hours because of having a dependent, longer waitlist time, and presumed better transplant success associated with being underweight rather than obese. The interviewee acknowledged the decision was difficult and weighed obesity and waitlist duration heavily.

Patient A was chosen because they have dependents and a decent life expectancy, despite patient B having a much longer life expectancy. The interviewee expressed a soft spot for people with dependents influenced by their own experience.

Patient B was chosen because they have been waiting longer (7 years vs. 5), have a dependent, and a good life expectancy (20 years). The interviewee weighed waiting time and dependents heavily and was uncertain about the impact of obesity and work hours.

Initially undecided due to conflict between dependents and longer life expectancy. Ultimately chose patient B for longer expected life despite having no dependents and shorter waitlist time. The interviewee acknowledged bias and difficulty in deciding.

The interviewee prioritized Patient A because they have dependents and have not committed serious crimes, emphasizing the responsibilities toward dependents outweigh the past criminal history of Patient B.
Patient B was prioritized due to longer waitlist time, greater expected post-transplant work hours, and urgency related to obesity, outweighing the slight difference in number of dependents.
Patient A was chosen despite fewer expected life years and less work hours because of having dependents and longer waiting time; the interviewee valued urgency and responsibility toward dependents.

Patient A is prioritized because they have been on the waiting list much longer (7 years versus 1 year), and it is less likely that Patient A will get another chance given the statistics of survival with 5 to 10 years life expectancy on dialysis. While Patient B has more dependents which would normally weigh in their favor, the chance of Patient B receiving another kidney soon is high, making Patient A's urgency more critical.

Patient B is chosen due to their greater ability to work more hours per week post-transplant, indicating better health and likelihood to maintain transplant success. Although Patient A has more dependents and longer expected years gained (25 vs. 20), Patient B's higher work capacity suggests better recovery and overall impact.

Patient B is ranked higher due to longer expected years gained and greater work hours per week post-transplant, indicating better health and recovery potential. Although Patient A has been on the waiting list longer and has dependents, the underweight status and lower expected life years lowers their priority because it predicts poor recovery and greater risk of transplant failure. The waitlist duration difference is less important here due to closeness in years.

Patient B was chosen due to significantly higher expected years of life gained from the transplant (25 vs 10), which the interviewee regarded as the most important factor, outweighing the longer waitlist time of Patient A.

Patient A was preferred due to higher years of life gained (25 vs 20), better obesity status (normal vs overweight), and shorter waitlist time (5 vs 7 years). Work hours and dependents were considered irrelevant in this decision.

Patient B was chosen due to higher expected years of life gained (20 vs 10) despite being obese. The interviewee considered obesity in context (e.g., muscular individuals may be classified obese) and viewed dependents and work hours as irrelevant. The shorter waitlist time (3 vs 5 years) was also not significant here.

Patient B is chosen due to having more dependents and being underweight, which is considered a healthier condition easier to improve compared to morbid obesity. Dependents add weight to the decision, and underweight is easier to rectify than obesity, leading to the belief that Patient B would derive more quality of life from the transplant.

Patient A is favored slightly due to having more dependents, a higher expected life gain, and being normal weight. Work hours and waitlist time are not considered relevant factors in the decision. The difference in dependents and weight favor Patient A enough to give them a slight advantage.

The decision is difficult and close. Patient B has a higher expected life gain but is obese. Patient A has more dependents but fewer years expected post-transplant and is underweight. The interviewee is conflicted, hoping Patient B will adopt a healthier lifestyle after transplant. Overall, there is a slight preference for Patient B but with low confidence due to ethical and moral complexities.
Chose patient B due to much longer waiting time on the transplant list, which was prioritized over number of dependents.
Chose patient B primarily due to longer waiting time despite fewer years of life gained and overweight status; quality of life considerations influenced decision.
Chose patient A mainly because they have been waiting longer; other factors like obesity and work hours were seen as balancing out.
Patient B chosen due to having more dependents, which affects more lives; despite Patient A's longer waitlist time, number of dependents weighed more heavily.
Patient A chosen because of considerably more years of life expected and more dependents, with better obesity level; work hours considered not important.
Patient A chosen despite shorter life expectancy because of having dependents and longer waitlist time; decision was difficult and conflicted by obesity level and years gained.
Patient A chosen for having dependents which are deemed more important than additional years of life gained by Patient B.
Patient A prioritized due to having more dependents, higher life expectancy, and normal weight, despite Patient B having longer wait time and more work hours; dependents considered most important.

Patient A favored for having dependents and longer waitlist time, despite Patient B's greater life expectancy and work hours; the trade-off is close and a numerical cutoff (e.g., 15 years per dependent) is considered.

The interviewee chose patient A because both patients are identical except obesity and waitlist years, and they believe underweight is equally risky as morbid obesity, so the deciding factor is who has been on the waiting list longer. Patient A has 7 years on the list versus patient B's 1 year, so patient A is chosen.

The interviewee favors patient B because patient B has been on the waiting list longer (7 vs 5 years). Although patient A has more dependents, greater life gained, and normal weight, patient B's longer wait time and expected commitment to losing weight (if possible) make them the preferred choice. The interviewee prioritizes waitlist years over other features.

The interviewee chooses patient A despite patient B having more expected life years gained, more work hours, and fewer dependents, because patient A has been on the waiting list longer. Obesity is not a relevant factor in this choice here, and the interviewee prioritizes waitlist years as the main consideration unless medical conditions would interfere with surgery.

Participant feels patient A should receive priority due to substantially longer expected years of life gained, despite patient B having more dependents; values direct impact on patient's life and prioritizes longevity over number of dependents, while acknowledging the difficulty of the decision.

Participant prioritizes patient A based on having more dependents and longer life expectancy, gives less importance to work hours and the fact that patient B has been on the waitlist longer; acknowledges obesity but considers it less significant than years of life and dependents; decision primarily guided by gut feeling.

Participant leans toward patient A due to having one dependent and longer waiting time on the list; places less weight on obesity and work hours, noting weight can change and work is not a major factor; views the decision as difficult given closer trade-offs between factors.

The participant prioritized Patient A because although Patient B would gain more life years, Patient A has been on the waiting list much longer (7 years vs 1 year), implying fewer future opportunities for transplant. The participant hopes Patient B might have more chances later. The decision was difficult and reflects weighing waitlist time over life years gained in this scenario.

The participant mostly disregarded work hours and number of dependents differences, considering dependents as a binary factor rather than by quantity. The main factors influencing the choice were years of life expected and years on the waitlist. Because the difference in waitlist years was small (5 vs 7), the participant leaned toward Patient A due to the greater expected life years gained (25 vs 20).

The participant discounted obesity and work hours as important factors. They noted Patient A has dependents and a longer wait time (5 vs 3 years), but the large difference in expected life years gained (20 vs 10) for Patient B was highly influential. Thus, Patient B was prioritized due to the significant life years advantage.

Patient A is prioritized because they have 3 dependents, which is considered the main factor for prioritization over Patient B, despite Patient B having been on the waiting list longer. The interviewee values family responsibilities more heavily than time spent waiting.

Patient A is chosen due to having more dependents, longer life expectancy, and healthier weight. Although Patient B would work more hours per week and has been on the waitlist longer, the interviewee prioritizes dependents, life expectancy, and healthy weight as the top three features.

The interviewee chooses Patient A, prioritizing number of dependents and obesity level (underweight favored over obese) over the greater years of life gained and higher expected work hours of Patient B. The interviewee notes that if Patient A had no dependents, they might have leaned towards Patient B, indicating some uncertainty.

The interviewee prioritizes patient A because they have dependents and assumes the dependents rely on patient A. They acknowledge the dilemma with life years gained but lean towards supporting dependents as the key factor.
Patient A is chosen due to having more dependents and expected longer life years. The interviewee also notes normal weight and fewer years on the waitlist for patient A as supportive factors.

Although patient B has more years of life gained and expected work hours, the interviewee struggles with the choice but ultimately prioritizes patient A due to having a dependent, despite feeling conflicted.
